step.title: Step 20
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 84, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 23 of about 48 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 20 actions returned the board to a state already seen 20 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 20 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Game over again! The purple bar grew to 1404 pixels (rows 35-63, cols 0-63) which is 29 rows × 64 cols = 1856... wait, 1404 pixels in a 64-wide area spanning rows 35-63 (29 rows) would be about 48% filled. But game_over=True.
So filling the bar too much causes game over! There must be a target range. The bar started at 2 pixels and game over happened at 1404 pixels. 
Wait, but the bar is at rows 35-63, which overlaps with the green blocks at rows 25-35. The bar grew upward into the green block area. Maybe the game over is triggered when the bar reaches a certain height or touches something.
Let me reset with ACTION7 and try a more careful approach. I need to find the right amount to fill the bar.
Actually, looking more carefully: the bar was at 205 pixels before I did 10 LEFT. Then it jumped to 1404 pixels. That's a huge increase from just 7 LEFT actions (only 7 executed, not 10, since game over stopped it).
Wait, the output says "After 10 LEFT" but only 7 actions were executed. The game over happened partway through. So the bar grew from 205 to 1404 in about 7 actions.
The key insight: the bar fills up as I move, and game over happens when it overfills. I need to find the target level.
Let me reset and try to fill it more carefully, checking after each batch.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole approach. Maybe the goal isn't to fill the bar at all. Maybe the goal is to navigate the agent to the purple targets at the top. The bar might just be a timer or progress indicator that counts down (or up) and causes game over if it reaches a limit.
Let me reset and try a completely different approach: navigate the agent to the upper corridor and reach one of the purple targets.
But how does the agent move vertically? The agent is in the lower corridor (rows 37-62). The upper corridor is at rows 7-23. There's no direct vertical path visible.
Wait - maybe MOUSE is used to "jump" between corridors. In level 1, MOUSE collected green blocks. In level 2, maybe MOUSE on the blue corridor areas teleports the agent?
Let me reset and try clicking on the upper blue corridor to teleport the agent there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game over from overfilling the bar. Need to reset and try different approach.
# The bar fills with movement - maybe it's a timer, not a goal meter.
# Goal might be to reach purple targets at top before bar fills up.

# Reset with ACTION7
result = action(['ACTION7'])
print(f"After ACTION7: game_over={last_action_result.get('game_over')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 6:
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Agent: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
    if n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] > 2:
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) > 30:
            print(f"Purple bar: {n['pixels']} pixels, rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Try MOUSE on upper blue corridor to teleport agent
result2 = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 15, 'col': 20}])
print(f"\nAfter MOUSE on upper corridor: game_over={last_action_result.get('game_over')}")

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 6:
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Agent: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After ACTION7: game_over=False
Agent: rows=37-40, cols=20-21

After MOUSE on upper corridor: game_over=False
Agent: rows=37-40, cols=20-21
